items.question_text: What is the primary purpose of the ATC system?
items.explanation: The ATC system's primary purpose is collision prevention.

choices.text: To prevent a collision involving aircraft operating in the system.
To provide additional services whenever they are requested.
To support National Security and Homeland Defense as its sole purpose.
To guarantee the most expeditious handling for every aircraft.

items.question_text: Which additional ATC-system purpose is listed besides collision prevention?
items.explanation: The paragraph adds safe, orderly, and expeditious flow plus support to National Security and Homeland Defense missions.

choices.text: Providing a safe, orderly, and expeditious flow of air traffic and supporting National Security and Homeland Defense missions.
Providing additional services without regard to workload or surveillance quality.
Exempting pilots from otherwise applicable regulations whenever ATC issues an instruction.
Providing ATC service to all model aircraft and all UAS below 400 feet AGL.

items.question_text: When must additional ATC services be provided?
items.explanation: They are required when the work situation permits.

choices.text: When the work situation permits.
Only when a pilot insists on receiving them.
Only when there are no aircraft conflicts anywhere in the facility.
Only when they are specifically listed as the primary purpose of the ATC system.

items.question_text: Which factor is specifically listed as one that may preclude the provision of additional services?
items.explanation: The paragraph lists several precluding factors, including volume of traffic, frequency congestion, quality of surveillance, workload, higher-priority duties, and the physical inability to scan and detect.

choices.text: The physical inability to scan and detect situations falling in that category.
A pilot's preference for a different phraseology style.
A requirement that all additional services be approved by the next facility.
The presence of any UAS anywhere in the NAS.

items.question_text: Controllers may deviate from the procedures and minima in this order if a deviation is necessary to assist an aircraft when an emergency has been declared.
items.explanation: Assisting an aircraft in a declared emergency is one of the listed reasons for deviation.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: To which UAS operations does the no-service provision in 2-1-1 apply?
items.explanation: The note limits the provision for other UAS to those operating entirely at or below 400 feet AGL.

choices.text: Other UAS operating entirely at or below 400 feet AGL.
All UAS at any altitude, without exception.
Only UAS that have filed an IFR flight plan.
Only public UAS operating above 400 feet AGL.

items.question_text: The ATC system's secondary purposes include:

choices.text: Providing a safe, orderly, and expeditious flow of air traffic and supporting National Security and Homeland Defense.
Issuing pilot certificates and medical clearances.
Managing airline scheduling and gate assignments.
Collecting landing fees and passenger taxes.

items.question_text: A controller may deviate from 7110.65 procedures when other procedures are prescribed in a letter of agreement.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: The provision of additional ATC services is required whenever a pilot requests them, regardless of workload.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: What gets first priority in the controller duty order?
items.explanation: The first-priority duty is separating aircraft and issuing required safety alerts.

choices.text: Separating aircraft and issuing required safety alerts.
Providing additional services before any other task.
Reporting suspicious activity before working conflicts.
Collecting weather reports before separation tasks.

items.question_text: If more than one action is required, what should be done first?
items.explanation: The action most critical from a safety standpoint is performed first.

choices.text: The action most critical from a safety standpoint.
The action that has been pending the longest.
The action preferred by the most senior pilot on frequency.
The action involving additional services, because it is optional and easier to close out first.

items.question_text: What kind of activity is specifically included in support to national security and homeland defense?
items.explanation: The paragraph specifically includes reporting suspicious and unusual aircraft or pilot activities.

choices.text: Reporting suspicious and unusual aircraft or pilot activities.
Providing all requested route shortcuts ahead of safety alerts.
Relieving pilots of the need to comply with weather-related regulations.
Automatically moving homeland-defense missions ahead of separation duties.

items.question_text: Controllers are responsible to become familiar with and stay aware of current weather information needed to perform ATC duties.
items.explanation: The note under 2-1-2c states that responsibility directly.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: When may controllers provide additional services to aircraft under 2-1-2?
items.explanation: Additional services come only to the extent possible and only after higher-priority duties and operational limitations are accounted for.

choices.text: To the extent possible, contingent on higher-priority duties and factors such as radar limits, traffic volume, frequency congestion, and workload.
Any time an aircraft requests them, regardless of operational limitations.
Only after the pilot has first filed an IFR flight plan.
Only when there is no need to use good judgment about the rest of the order.

items.question_text: A controller has three tasks: separate converging aircraft, report a suspicious aircraft to the supervisor, and provide weather to a pilot. The correct order is:

choices.text: 1. Separate aircraft, 2. Report suspicious aircraft, 3. Provide weather.
1. Report suspicious aircraft, 2. Separate aircraft, 3. Provide weather.
1. Provide weather, 2. Separate aircraft, 3. Report.
Handle in whatever order the requests arrived.

items.question_text: The controller should perform the most safety-critical action first, even if it means delaying a national security report.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: When should automation procedures be preferred over nonautomation procedures?
items.explanation: Automation is preferred when workload, communications, and equipment capabilities permit.

choices.text: When workload, communications, and equipment capabilities permit.
Only when radar separation is unavailable.
Only during closed-loop clearances for TBM.
Whenever a pilot asks for a vector.

items.question_text: For time-based management when workload permits, which kind of automation clearances should be preferred?
items.explanation: Closed loop clearances are preferred to open loop clearances for TBM advantage.

choices.text: Closed loop clearances.
Open loop clearances.
Voice-only clearances without automation support.
Nonradar clearances regardless of workload.

items.question_text: When should radar separation be preferred to nonradar separation?
items.explanation: Radar separation is preferred when it offers operational advantage and the conditions permit it.

choices.text: When it provides an operational advantage and workload, communications, and equipment permit.
Whenever it is available, regardless of operational advantage.
Only when TBM closed loop clearances are also being used.
Only when nonradar separation would result in less vectoring.

items.question_text: Nonradar separation may be preferred to radar separation when the situation dictates that an operational advantage will be gained.
items.explanation: The paragraph explicitly permits preferring nonradar separation when it produces an operational advantage.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: What is one example of a situation where nonradar separation may provide the operational advantage?
items.explanation: The note gives vertical separation that avoids excessive vectoring as an example.

choices.text: When vertical separation would preclude excessive vectoring.
Whenever a pilot prefers not to hear a radar vector.
Whenever open loop clearances are unavailable.
Whenever workload is low, regardless of the operational result.

items.question_text: A controller is vectoring an aircraft for approach. Radar is operational but using it would require 5 heading changes. Vertical separation would allow a direct descent. Which should be used?

choices.text: Nonradar (vertical) separation — it provides operational advantage by avoiding excessive vectoring.
Radar separation — it's always preferred when available.
Either method — controller's choice.
Ask the pilot which they prefer.

items.question_text: Nonradar separation may be preferred over radar when operational advantage will be gained.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: What is the baseline service order in 2-1-4 before the listed priority exceptions are applied?
items.explanation: Controllers provide ATC service on a first-come, first-served basis as circumstances permit, except for the listed priority cases.

choices.text: First come, first served as circumstances permit.
IFR first, then VFR, then SVFR in every case.
Medical flights first, then all other flights alphabetically.
Traffic flow first, even when it compromises safety.

items.question_text: Which aircraft has the right of way over all other air traffic?
items.explanation: An aircraft in distress has the right of way over all other air traffic.

choices.text: An aircraft in distress.
Any IFR aircraft that has retained its IFR flight plan.
Any aircraft with MEDEVAC in the remarks section.
Any diverted flight using DVRSN, regardless of circumstances.

items.question_text: When does a civil air ambulance flight receive priority handling as MEDEVAC?
items.explanation: The pilot must verbally identify the flight as MEDEVAC followed by the FAA-authorized call sign or the full civil registration.

choices.text: When the pilot verbally identifies the flight by saying MEDEVAC followed by the FAA-authorized call sign or the full civil registration.
Whenever the flight plan contains the letter L in remarks, even if the pilot never says anything on frequency.
Whenever the aircraft files IFR and requests the shortest route.
Whenever the aircraft is carrying any passenger with a medical appointment.

items.question_text: AIR EVAC and HOSP flights receive priority handling when the pilot verbally requests it.
items.explanation: Verbal pilot request is the operational trigger for AIR EVAC and HOSP priority handling.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: What must be true before Garden Plot flights receive priority handling?
items.explanation: Garden Plot priority is provided only when CARF notifies that such priority is authorized.

choices.text: CARF must notify that such priority is authorized.
The pilot must add Garden Plot to the flight ID in the flight plan.
The aircraft must also be flying as MEDEVAC or SCOOT.
The destination tower must verbally approve the route first.

items.question_text: When NAOC is used in air/ground communications or the flight is identified by landline notification, what should be done with requests from that aircraft?
items.explanation: Requests from a NAOC aircraft should be approved as soon as practicable.

choices.text: Approve any request as soon as practicable.
Treat the aircraft as ordinary first-come, first-served traffic unless the flight ID itself contains NAOC.
Approve only altitude changes, because route changes are excluded from NAOC handling.
Wait for the aircraft to restate NAOC as part of the flight ID in the flight plan before taking priority action.

items.question_text: IFR aircraft retain priority over VFR aircraft solely because they have kept an IFR flight plan active.
items.explanation: Retaining an IFR flight plan does not by itself create priority over VFR aircraft; the paragraph instead specifically gives IFR aircraft priority over SVFR aircraft.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: A MEDEVAC flight has 'MEDEVAC' in the flight plan remarks but the pilot has not stated it verbally. Does this aircraft get priority handling?

choices.text: No — verbal identification by the pilot stating 'MEDEVAC' followed by the call sign is required. Flight plan notations are informational only.
Yes — the flight plan notation triggers automatic priority.
Yes — but only after the controller verifies with the company.
No — MEDEVAC priority is only for military aircraft.

items.question_text: An IFR aircraft on a 5-mile final and a VFR aircraft entering the pattern: who has priority?

choices.text: Neither — IFR retention does not afford priority over VFR. The aircraft must sequence naturally.
The IFR aircraft — IFR always has priority over VFR.
The VFR aircraft — they were in the pattern first.
The IFR aircraft because it's on final approach.

items.question_text: When should the word "immediately" be used?
items.explanation: Immediately is used only when expeditious compliance is required to avoid an imminent situation.

choices.text: Only when expeditious compliance is required to avoid an imminent situation.
Any time prompt compliance is useful but the situation is still only developing.
Whenever a pilot appears slow to respond to any ordinary instruction.
Whenever the controller wants to sound urgent on frequency.

items.question_text: When should the word "expedite" be used?
items.explanation: Expedite is for prompt compliance needed to avoid the development of an imminent situation.

choices.text: Only when prompt compliance is required to avoid the development of an imminent situation.
Only when expeditious compliance is already required to avoid an imminent situation.
Whenever a new altitude is assigned, because all climbs and descents are urgent.
Only during emergency declarations.

items.question_text: If ATC issues an expedite climb or descent and later changes or restates the altitude without saying expedite again, what happens to the expedite instruction?
items.explanation: The expedite instruction is canceled when the altitude is changed or restated without a renewed expedite instruction.

choices.text: It is canceled.
It remains in effect until the aircraft reaches the originally assigned altitude.
It automatically escalates to immediately.
It transfers to any future altitude assignment unless the pilot refuses it.

items.question_text: If time permits, should the controller include the reason for using immediately or expedite?
items.explanation: The paragraph says to include the reason if time permits in either case.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Which urgency word fits a clearance when prompt compliance is needed to avoid the development of an imminent situation, but the situation is not yet at the stronger threshold?
items.explanation: That description matches expedite rather than immediately.

choices.text: Expedite.
Immediately.
Unable.
Negative.

items.question_text: A controller issues a descent clearance. After 20 seconds, the pilot has not responded. What should the controller do?

choices.text: Reissue the clearance — the pilot may not have received it. If still no response, take action to maintain separation.
File a pilot deviation immediately.
Wait another 60 seconds — pilots have unlimited response time.
Assume the pilot is complying silently.

items.question_text: When must a controller issue a safety alert?
items.explanation: A safety alert is issued when the controller is aware the aircraft is in unsafe proximity to terrain, obstructions, or other aircraft.

choices.text: When the controller is aware the aircraft is in unsafe proximity to terrain, obstructions, or other aircraft.
Only when the pilot requests one.
Only when the aircraft has already violated a minimum altitude.
Only when another controller transfers the responsibility for issuing it.

items.question_text: When may a controller discontinue further safety alerts?
items.explanation: Further alerts may stop once the pilot informs the controller that action is being taken to resolve the situation.

choices.text: When the pilot informs the controller that action is being taken to resolve the situation.
As soon as the alert is transmitted once, regardless of pilot response.
Only after technical operations confirms the terrain or conflict source.
Only after a different controller takes radar responsibility for the aircraft.

items.question_text: What source may lead to recognition of an unsafe proximity situation requiring a safety alert?
items.explanation: The note lists several possible recognition sources, including MSAW or E-MSAW, automatic altitude readouts, conflict or Mode C intruder alerts, PAR observations, and pilot reports.

choices.text: Any of the listed sources, such as MSAW/E-MSAW, automatic altitude readouts, conflict or Mode C intruder alerts, PAR observations, or pilot reports.
Only a pilot report, because automation cannot be used to recognize unsafe proximity.
Only PAR observations, because they are the only recognized alert source in the paragraph.
Only a conflict alert involving a target already under the controller's separation responsibility.

items.question_text: A controller may assume another position has already observed an unsafe situation and therefore does not need to issue a safety alert.
items.explanation: The paragraph expressly says not to assume someone else already saw the problem and issued the alert.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Complete the low-altitude alert phraseology: "CHECK YOUR ALTITUDE _____."
items.explanation: The phraseology ends with the word IMMEDIATELY.

choices.text: IMMEDIATELY

items.question_text: Who decides what course of action will be taken after a safety alert is issued?
items.explanation: Once the alert is issued, it is solely the pilot's prerogative to determine what action, if any, will be taken.

choices.text: The pilot.
The technical operations specialist monitoring the alerting system.
The controller who first recognized the unsafe proximity.
The supervisor coordinating the sector response.

items.question_text: A controller must issue a safety alert even if it means interrupting another transmission.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Why are military procedures prescribed as additions, modifications, and exceptions to FAA procedure?
items.explanation: They are prescribed when a common procedure has not been attained or to fulfill a specific requirement.

choices.text: When a common procedure has not been attained or to fulfill a specific requirement.
Whenever a military unit prefers local wording instead of FAA procedure.
Only when civil traffic is not present.
Whenever a military facility wants to ignore a standing LOA temporarily.

items.question_text: Which facilities must apply the procedures of the military service that operates them?
items.explanation: Facilities operated by that military service apply its procedures.

choices.text: ATC facilities operated by that military service.
Only FAA-operated facilities serving mixed-use airports.
Any facility whenever a military aircraft checks in first.
Only facilities specifically named in a NOTAM.

items.question_text: If a facility exclusively supports a designated military airport, what determines which military procedures apply?
items.explanation: The designation of the supported military airport determines which military procedures apply.

choices.text: The designation of the military airport being exclusively supported.
The parent organization of the facility, regardless of the airport it supports.
The first aircraft type served each day.
The nearest military unit's preferred checklist.

items.question_text: Other ATC facilities may apply military procedures when specified in a letter of agreement.
items.explanation: Subparagraph c explicitly allows that application path.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: At an FAA facility that provides approach service to both a Naval Air Station and a civil airport, what procedures does the example say are applied at both locations?
items.explanation: The example says the FAA facility applies basic FAA procedures at both locations.

choices.text: Basic FAA procedures.
USN procedures at the military field and FAA procedures at the civil field.
USAF procedures because the facility is civilian.
Any procedures chosen by the local watch supervisor for the shift.

items.question_text: Civilian controllers are required to apply military procedures prescribed in the 7110.65 when handling military aircraft under those procedures.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: What is the primary purpose of the ATC system?

choices.text: To prevent a collision involving aircraft operating in the system.
To provide the most expeditious movement for every aircraft regardless of separation needs.
To furnish additional services whenever requested, regardless of workload.
To support National Security and Homeland Defense as the system's only mission.

items.explanation: Collision prevention is the ATC system's primary purpose.

items.question_text: When the work situation permits, controllers must provide additional ATC services; those services are not optional.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Additional services are required when the work situation permits, although factors like traffic, workload, and surveillance limits may preclude them.

items.question_text: If a deviation is necessary to assist an aircraft when an emergency has been declared, controllers may deviate from the procedures and minima in this order.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: An emergency is one of the listed conditions that allows deviation from the order's normal procedures and minima.

items.question_text: This no-service limitation applies to model aircraft operating in the NAS at any altitude and to other UAS only when they are operating entirely at or below 400 feet AGL.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The note extends the model-aircraft limitation to any altitude and limits the other-UAS provision to operations entirely at or below 400 feet AGL.

items.situation: A pilot of a UAS operating at 350 feet AGL requests traffic advisories. What do you do?

choices.text: Inform the pilot that ATC services are not provided for UAS operating at or below 400 feet AGL.
Provide traffic advisories as requested.
Tell the pilot to climb to 500 feet for service.
Hand off to the FSS for UAS coordination.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-1(e): ATC services are not provided for UAS at or below 400ft AGL.

items.question_text: When must a controller issue a safety alert?

choices.text: When the controller is aware the aircraft's position or altitude places it in unsafe proximity to terrain, obstructions, or other aircraft.
Only after the pilot asks whether the situation looks unsafe.
Only if the controller is already responsible for separation from every aircraft involved.
Only if an automated alerting system displays the warning first.

items.explanation: The trigger is controller awareness that the aircraft is in unsafe proximity to terrain, obstructions, or other aircraft.

items.question_text: Controllers must remain vigilant for situations of unsafe aircraft proximity and issue a safety alert when the situation is recognized.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: If the pilot informs you that action is being taken to resolve the unsafe situation, you may discontinue the issuance of further safety alerts.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Further alerts may stop once the pilot says action is being taken to resolve the problem.

items.question_text: This safety-alert responsibility applies even when another controller has responsibility for the aircraft; you do not assume the unsafe situation has already been observed and alerted.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph specifically says not to assume someone else already saw the problem and issued the alert.

items.situation: You observe an aircraft at 3,000 feet heading toward a 3,200-foot tower, 2 miles ahead. The aircraft is not under your jurisdiction but you can see the conflict on radar. What must you do?

choices.text: Issue a safety alert immediately — do not assume the responsible controller has observed the situation.
Contact the responsible controller first and wait for their action.
Nothing — the aircraft is under another controller's jurisdiction.
Monitor the situation and issue an alert only if the conflict becomes imminent.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-6: Issue a safety alert if you are aware an aircraft is in unsafe proximity to terrain or obstructions. Do not assume another controller has observed it.

items.situation: An aircraft is dangerously low and you need to issue a low-altitude alert. Build the transmission.
items.target_phrase: LOW ALTITUDE ALERT CESSNA THREE FOUR JULIETT CHECK YOUR ALTITUDE IMMEDIATELY

items.question_text: What should a controller determine first when a pilot reports an inflight equipment malfunction?

choices.text: The nature and extent of any special handling desired.
Whether the pilot wants to cancel IFR immediately.
Which maintenance office should receive the final report after landing.
Whether another sector can take the aircraft right away.

items.explanation: The first step is understanding the malfunction and the special handling the pilot wants.

items.question_text: Controllers must provide the maximum assistance possible consistent with equipment, workload, and any special handling requested when a pilot reports an inflight equipment malfunction.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Maximum assistance is required, but it remains bounded by available equipment, workload, and the specific handling requested.

items.question_text: If another controller or facility will subsequently handle the aircraft, controllers must relay all pertinent details about the malfunction and any special handling required or already being provided.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The information has to follow the aircraft so later controllers understand both the malfunction and the handling plan.

items.question_text: This malfunction-handling rule applies to partial or complete equipment failures that may affect safety, separation, IFR continuation, or RVSM capability in the ATC system.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The note defines inflight equipment malfunctions broadly enough to include those operational effects.

items.situation: A pilot reports 'Center, we've lost our DME.' The aircraft is at FL350, 150 NM from destination, in VMC. What must you determine?

choices.text: The nature and extent of any special handling the pilot desires — ask what they need.
Immediately vector the aircraft to the nearest airport.
No action — DME failure is not critical in VMC.
Declare an emergency — DME failure is always an emergency.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-7(a): Determine the nature and extent of special handling desired. Do not assume.

items.question_text: What does a pilot's use of the term "minimum fuel" indicate?

choices.text: The fuel supply has reached a state where, upon reaching destination, the pilot cannot accept any undue delay.
An emergency already exists and traffic priority must be provided immediately.
The pilot is canceling IFR and requesting direct routing to the nearest airport.
The aircraft has no legal fuel reserve remaining and must land before any other traffic.

items.explanation: Minimum fuel is an advisory about inability to accept undue delay, not an automatic emergency declaration.

items.question_text: When an aircraft declares minimum fuel, controllers must inform any facility receiving control jurisdiction of the minimum-fuel problem and remain alert for any delay en route.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Both the handoff notification and delay awareness are explicit controller actions in the paragraph.

items.question_text: If the remaining usable fuel supply suggests the need for traffic priority to ensure a safe landing, the pilot should declare an emergency and report fuel remaining in minutes.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The note distinguishes minimum fuel from the point where a pilot should move to an emergency declaration with fuel remaining in minutes.

items.question_text: This minimum-fuel advisory rule applies as a warning of possible future emergency and does not by itself imply a need for traffic priority.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Minimum fuel is not itself an emergency or automatic priority request, though it warns that an emergency may develop if undue delay occurs.

items.situation: An aircraft declares minimum fuel while 50 NM from destination. You are about to hand off to the approach controller who is running 10-mile spacing on final. What TWO things must you do?

choices.text: Inform the approach controller of the minimum fuel status AND be alert for any occurrence that might delay the aircraft.
Just hand off normally — approach control will handle it.
Declare an emergency — minimum fuel requires immediate diversion.
Tell the pilot to declare an emergency if they want priority.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-8: Inform the receiving facility and be alert for delays. Minimum fuel is NOT an emergency but requires proactive action.

items.question_text: What kinds of information should be reported as soon as possible under 2-1-9?

choices.text: Information about NAS components or flight conditions that may have an adverse effect on air safety.
Only equipment outages that have already been classified as a NOTAM by FSS.
Only conditions that directly affect IFR operations, not general air safety.
Only maintenance issues reported by technical operations personnel.

items.explanation: The rule is broad: any NAS component information or flight condition that may adversely affect air safety is reported as soon as possible.

items.question_text: Report as soon as possible any information concerning NAS components or flight conditions that may have an adverse effect on air safety.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That directive is the core instruction of the paragraph.

items.question_text: Controllers must report the information to the appropriate FSS, airport manager's office, ARTCC, approach control facility, operations office, or military operations office.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The receiving office depends on the situation, but the paragraph lists the appropriate reporting targets.

items.question_text: This reporting rule applies to both NAS-component information and flight-condition information when either may adversely affect air safety.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph covers both kinds of safety-relevant information, not just one category.

items.situation: You observe that the runway edge lights on Runway 18 are flickering intermittently. No pilot has reported it. What must you do?

choices.text: Report it as soon as possible to the appropriate facility — this is a NAS component that may adversely affect air safety.
Wait for a pilot to report it before taking action.
Note it in the shift log for the next controller.
Only report it if the lights fail completely.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-9: Report information concerning NAS components that may adversely affect air safety as soon as possible.

items.question_text: What should be done first when an aircraft reports a ground-based NAVAID malfunction?

Shut the aid down immediately and advise all traffic it is unusable.
Activate standby equipment before obtaining any corroborating report.
Inform technical operations first and wait for instructions before doing anything else.

items.question_text: When a GPS or WAAS anomaly is reported, controllers must record at least the aircraft make, model, and call sign; the location or position and altitude where the anomaly was observed; and the date and time of occurrence.

items.question_text: If a second aircraft confirms a reported ground-based NAVAID malfunction, or if no second report is available, controllers must activate the standby equipment or request the monitor facility to activate it.

items.question_text: This malfunction-handling rule applies both to reported ground-based NAVAID malfunctions and to reported GPS or WAAS anomalies, with separate follow-up actions for each.

False

items.explanation: Subparagraph a covers ground-based NAVAIDs, while subparagraphs b and c cover GPS and WAAS anomalies.

items.situation: An aircraft reports the ABC VOR is unreliable. You ask a second aircraft to check. The second aircraft reports the VOR is working normally. What should you do?

choices.text: Continue using the VOR and inform the first aircraft that the second reported normal operations.

items.situation: GPS has been reported unreliable in the area 30 miles south of Waco VOR. Build the advisory broadcast.
items.target_phrase: ATTENTION ALL AIRCRAFT GPS REPORTED UNRELIABLE IN THE AREA THIRTY MILES SOUTH OF WACO V O R

items.explanation: Terms of MARSA assign responsibility and provide separation among participating aircraft; ATC's role is with respect to nonparticipating IFR traffic.

items.situation: Two F-16s request to conduct formation practice. The flight lead states 'we'll take MARSA.' There is no LOA for this operation. Can MARSA be applied?

choices.text: No — MARSA requires prior authorization in an LOA or appropriate FAA/military document.
Yes — the pilot verbally assumed responsibility for separation.
Yes — MARSA applies automatically to all military formations.
Yes — but only if the supervisor approves it.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-11(a): MARSA may only be applied to operations specified in an LOA or appropriate document.

items.question_text: How are formation flights controlled under 2-1-13?

choices.text: As a single aircraft.
As separate aircraft at all times, regardless of formation status.
As a single aircraft only after beacon codes are consolidated.
As separate aircraft during join-up but as a single aircraft only after departure from controlled airspace.

items.explanation: Formation flights are controlled as a single aircraft.

items.question_text: For a standard formation, only the aircraft acting as the lead must squawk the ATC-assigned beacon code, and all other aircraft must squawk standby.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is the standard-formation beacon-code rule after join-up.

items.question_text: If two aircraft request a formation join-up, controllers may support the join-up only when requested by a participating pilot, all participating pilots concur, and either participating pilot reports the other in sight.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: All three listed conditions must be met for the two-aircraft join-up support case.

items.question_text: This formation-flight rule places separation responsibility between aircraft within the formation on the flight leader and other formation pilots, including the transition periods during join-up and breakaway.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Internal formation separation remains with the flight leader and participating pilots, including transition periods.

items.situation: A formation of four F-16s is on approach. The flight leader reports they are beginning a transition from formation flight to individual control. The aircraft will maneuver to separate from each other. What is the controller's role?

choices.text: Continue controlling the formation as a single aircraft — the flight leader is responsible for separation within the formation during transition.
Issue separation instructions to each aircraft individually.
Tell the formation to abort the transition.
Hand off each aircraft to a different controller.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-13: Formation flights are controlled as a single aircraft. Separation within the formation rests with the flight leader, including during transition periods.

items.question_text: What must be ensured before you allow an aircraft under your control to enter another controller's area of jurisdiction?

choices.text: That the necessary coordination has been accomplished.
That the receiving controller has already issued the next clearance directly to the pilot.
That the aircraft has changed frequency even if no coordination has occurred.
That the pilot prefers the route change, even if affected controllers have not been told.

items.explanation: Necessary coordination is completed before allowing entry into another controller's area.

items.question_text: Before issuing a control instruction directly to a pilot that changes heading, route, speed, or altitude, controllers must ensure coordination is completed with all controllers whose areas of jurisdiction are affected unless otherwise specified by an LOA or facility directive.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Affected controllers must be covered by coordination before the direct instruction is issued unless an LOA or facility directive says otherwise.

items.question_text: If your control instruction will be relayed to the pilot through an FSS, New York Radio, San Francisco Radio, another pilot, or another non-radar-controller source, you are still responsible for ensuring all required coordination is completed.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Relaying the instruction through another source does not remove the issuing controller's responsibility for coordination.

items.question_text: This coordination rule applies both to entry into another controller's area of jurisdiction and to direct pilot instructions that affect other controllers' areas.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph covers both the airspace-entry case and the direct-control-instruction case.

items.situation: An aircraft under your control is 5 miles from the adjacent sector boundary. You haven't coordinated the handoff yet. The receiving controller is busy. Can the aircraft cross the boundary?

choices.text: No — coordination must be completed BEFORE the aircraft enters the other controller's jurisdiction.
Yes — the receiving controller will adapt.
Yes — but advise the aircraft to squawk standby.
Yes — if the aircraft reports the boundary crossing.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-14(a): Ensure necessary coordination has been accomplished BEFORE allowing entry.

items.question_text: Under what conditions is control of an aircraft transferred?

choices.text: At a prescribed or coordinated location, time, fix, or altitude, or at completion of a radar handoff and frequency change when authorized by an LOA or facility directive.
Whenever the aircraft is nearing a sector boundary, regardless of whether coordination conditions are met.
Only after the receiving controller issues a new clearance directly to the aircraft.
Only when the pilot requests a different sector or ARTCC.

items.explanation: Those are the listed control-transfer conditions.

items.question_text: Controllers must transfer control of an aircraft only after eliminating any potential conflict with other aircraft for which they have separation responsibility.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Potential conflicts with aircraft still under your separation responsibility are removed before control is transferred.

items.question_text: If specific coordination has not occurred and no LOA or facility directive says otherwise, a controller must assume control of an aircraft only after it is in that controller's area of jurisdiction.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Early assumption of control requires specific coordination or other specified authorization.

items.question_text: This control-transfer rule applies both to when transferring control away and to when assuming control from another controller.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph addresses conditions for both giving up and taking on control.

items.situation: Controller A initiates a radar handoff to Controller B. The data block is flashing on B's scope but B hasn't accepted it yet. A tells the aircraft to contact B's frequency. The pilot changes frequency. Has control been properly transferred?

choices.text: No — both the radar handoff AND frequency change must be COMPLETED. B hadn't accepted the handoff yet.
Yes — the pilot changed frequency so transfer is complete.
Yes — initiating the handoff is sufficient.
Yes — but only because A instructed the frequency change.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-15(a)(2): Transfer requires BOTH radar handoff AND frequency change to be complete.

items.question_text: Before issuing a clearance that would require flight within a surface area for which a nonapproach control tower has responsibility, what must be done?

choices.text: Coordinate on an individual aircraft basis with the appropriate nonapproach control tower unless otherwise specified by LOA.
Obtain direct authorization from the pilot because tower coordination is optional for surface-area entry.
Transfer communications first and let the tower decide whether the clearance may be issued afterward.
Wait until the aircraft is already inside the surface area so the local controller can observe it directly.

items.explanation: The paragraph requires individual-aircraft coordination with the appropriate nonapproach control tower unless an LOA says otherwise.

items.question_text: Controllers must transfer communications to the appropriate facility, if required, prior to operation within a surface area for which the tower has responsibility.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: If communication transfer is required, it happens before the operation within the tower-responsibility surface area.

items.question_text: If you are providing radar traffic advisory service to an aircraft that will enter another facility's airspace, you must coordinate with the appropriate control tower for transit authorization.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Transit authorization is coordinated with the appropriate tower in that radar-advisory-entry case.

items.question_text: This surface-area rule applies to coordination before clearances, to transit authorization when radar advisory service will enter another facility's airspace, and to communication transfer before tower-responsibility surface-area operations.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph covers those three related coordination and communication responsibilities.

items.situation: You need to vector an IFR arrival through the tower's surface area at 1,500 feet to join the approach. Your facility has an LOA with the tower that waives individual coordination for IFR arrivals. What should you do?

choices.text: Issue the vector without individual coordination — the LOA waives the requirement.
Coordinate with the tower for this specific aircraft.
Hand the aircraft off to the tower.
The aircraft cannot enter a surface area at any altitude.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-16(a): Individual coordination is required UNLESS otherwise specified in an LOA.

items.question_text: What is normally specified when transferring radio communications?

choices.text: The facility or location name and terminal function, the frequency if not omitted under a listed exception, and if required the time, fix, altitude, or specifically when to contact.
Only the new frequency, because facility names and contact timing are always optional.
Only the receiving controller's initials and the aircraft call sign.
Only the frequency and the words "change approved."

items.explanation: The paragraph lists the transfer elements: who to contact, which frequency to use unless an omission exception applies, and if required when to make contact.

items.question_text: Controllers must, within a reasonable amount of time, take appropriate action to establish or restore communications with aircraft for which a communications transfer or initial contact to their sector is expected or required.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That follow-up duty applies when expected communications do not occur.

items.question_text: If an operational advantage will be gained and coordination is completed with the receiving controller, you may instruct an aircraft on the ground to monitor the receiving controller's frequency.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Monitor instructions on the ground are allowed only when there is an operational advantage and the receiving controller has been coordinated with.

items.question_text: This frequency-change-avoidance rule applies to helicopters known to be single-piloted during air-taxiing, hovering, or low-level flight whenever necessary control instructions can be relayed until the pilot is able to change frequency.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The scope of the helicopter caution is limited to that operational context and those pilots.

items.situation: An aircraft was transferred to your sector and should have checked in by now. Five minutes have passed since the aircraft entered your area of jurisdiction and came within radio coverage. Another controller says there is no need to act yet because the transfer was already completed and the pilot will call eventually.
items.para_context: Controllers must take appropriate action within a reasonable amount of time to establish or restore communications with aircraft expected to contact their sector. A reasonable amount of time is considered 5 minutes from entry into the area of jurisdiction or coming within radio coverage.

choices.text: Take appropriate action now to establish or restore communications rather than waiting indefinitely for the pilot to call.
Do nothing until the aircraft has been silent for at least 15 minutes because the transfer itself satisfied the rule.
Assume the aircraft remained on the previous controller's frequency intentionally unless the pilot declares radio failure.
Wait for the next sector to try to contact the aircraft because the reasonable-time rule applies only to initial departure contact.

items.explanation: The paragraph creates an active follow-up duty once expected communications have not occurred within a reasonable amount of time.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-17(b): Transfer communications by specifying facility name and frequency.

items.question_text: What are the allowed verbal response types to an operational request?

choices.text: Approve it, approve it with restrictions, state unable with a reason if time permits, or state stand by.
Approve it silently, deny it only in writing, or defer it to the receiving controller.
Repeat the request back exactly and wait for the requester to decide whether it is approved.
Use only approved as requested or unable; stand by and restricted approval are not authorized responses.

items.explanation: The paragraph lists four response paths: approved, restricted approval, unable, and stand by.

items.question_text: If time permits and you cannot grant an operational request, you should state unable and provide a reason and or additional instructions as necessary.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Unable may be followed by a reason or additional instructions when time permits.

items.question_text: This operational-request response rule applies to requests from another controller, a pilot, or a vehicle operator.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The opening sentence names those three requester categories explicitly.

items.situation: A pilot requests a route amendment that you cannot approve yet because another coordination task is still pending. Another controller says you should say "approved" now so the pilot knows the request was heard and then fix the details later if needed.
items.para_context: Stand by is neither approval nor denial; it acknowledges the request and tells the requester a response will come later. Approved and unable have their own distinct meanings and uses.

choices.text: Use stand by to acknowledge the request while withholding approval until you can actually respond.
Say approved now because any acknowledgment counts as provisional approval.
Say unable permanently because a request that cannot be answered immediately must always be denied.
Remain silent until you can approve it, because stand by is considered an improper response to operational requests.

items.explanation: Stand by exists specifically to acknowledge a request without approving or denying it immediately.

items.instruction: Build the correct phraseology from these tiles.
items.target_phrase: APPROVED AS REQUESTED
items.explanation: Per 2-1-18(a): Standard phraseology for approving an operational request.

items.question_text: When are wake turbulence procedures applied?

choices.text: To an aircraft operating behind another aircraft when wake turbulence separation is required.
To every aircraft operating behind any other aircraft regardless of whether wake turbulence separation is required.
Only after the pilot reports feeling wake turbulence.
Only during visual approaches because IFR aircraft are handled by other minima rules.

items.explanation: Wake turbulence procedures are tied to situations where wake turbulence separation is required.

items.question_text: Wake turbulence separation minima must continue to touchdown for all IFR aircraft not making a visual approach or maintaining visual separation.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph explicitly carries the wake minima to touchdown for those IFR aircraft.

items.question_text: If an IFR aircraft is not making a visual approach and is not maintaining visual separation, wake turbulence separation minima must continue all the way to touchdown.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That conditional phrasing is just the rule restated in its operational trigger form.

items.question_text: This wake-turbulence rule applies to aircraft operating behind another aircraft when wake separation is required and specifically addresses continuation of minima to touchdown for certain IFR aircraft.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph has both the general application statement and the touchdown-continuation rule for IFR aircraft.

items.situation: A B737 is on a 5-mile final behind a B757 that landed 2 minutes ago. Required wake turbulence separation is 4 miles. Current radar shows the B737 at 4.5 miles from the threshold. Can you clear the B737 to land?

choices.text: Ensure the B737 maintains at least 4 miles of separation until the threshold — wake turbulence separation must be maintained throughout the approach.
Clear the B737 to land — 4.5 miles exceeds the minimum.
Wake turbulence is not a factor after landing.
Ask the B737 pilot if they want extra spacing.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-19(a): Wake turbulence procedures are mandatory when separation is required. They are not advisory.

items.question_text: When must wake turbulence cautionary advisories be issued to aircraft operating behind traffic requiring wake turbulence separation?

choices.text: When listed terminal or IFR visual conditions exist, and also whenever in the controller's judgment wake turbulence may have an adverse effect on an aircraft.
Only when the pilot specifically requests the advisory by name.
Only after a wake encounter has already been reported.
Only for super aircraft, because heavy-aircraft wake is covered by mandatory separation alone.

items.explanation: The paragraph gives both specified cases and a broader judgment-based caution advisory duty.

items.question_text: When traffic is known to be a Super aircraft, controllers must include the word Super in the wake turbulence cautionary advisory, and when traffic is known to be Heavy, they must include the word Heavy.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The advisory includes Super or Heavy in the traffic description when known.

items.question_text: If an IFR aircraft accepts a visual approach or visual separation behind traffic that requires wake turbulence separation, controllers must issue a wake turbulence cautionary advisory.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That IFR visual-acceptance case is one of the specifically listed advisory triggers.

items.question_text: This cautionary-advisory rule applies to specified aircraft operating behind traffic requiring wake turbulence separation and also to any aircraft that, in the controller's judgment, may be adversely affected by wake turbulence.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph includes both named trigger categories and a broader judgment-based cautionary advisory rule.

items.situation: A B757 departed 2 minutes ago heading north. A C172 is departing on a crossing runway heading east. What must you include in the wake turbulence advisory?

choices.text: The B757's position, altitude if known, and direction of flight (north).
Only the aircraft type — B757.
Only 'caution wake turbulence.'
The advisory is optional — the C172 pilot can see the B757.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-20(a): Wake turbulence advisories must include position, altitude if known, and direction of flight.

items.target_phrase: CAUTION WAKE TURBULENCE HEAVY BOEING SEVEN FORTY SEVEN DEPARTING OFF YOUR LEFT

items.question_text: When are traffic advisories issued to aircraft on your frequency?

choices.text: Unless the aircraft is in Class A airspace or the pilot requested omission, when in your judgment proximity may diminish below the applicable separation minima or otherwise warrants the advisory where no minima apply.
Only when a pilot explicitly asks for every traffic call regardless of airspace or proximity.
Only to IFR aircraft because VFR pilots are responsible for all traffic avoidance without advisory support.
Only when separation has already been lost and radar vectors are the next required step.

items.explanation: The paragraph states the default service, the exceptions, and the judgment-based trigger where no separation minima apply.

items.question_text: If traffic you have issued is not reported in sight, controllers must inform the pilot when that traffic is no factor or no longer observed on radar.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph requires a follow-up when previously issued traffic is not reported in sight and later becomes no factor or no longer observed.

items.question_text: If a pilot requests radar vectors to assist in avoiding traffic, controllers must provide them when the aircraft is within their area of jurisdiction or coordination has been effected with the sector or facility whose area the aircraft is operating in, and if unable they must inform the pilot.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The vector-assistance provision is conditional on jurisdiction or coordination, and inability must be communicated.

items.question_text: This traffic-advisory rule applies differently to radar-identified aircraft, non-radar-identified aircraft, and Mode C aircraft that are not radar identified, with content tailored to the information available in each case.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph has separate advisory content rules for those categories.

items.situation: Two aircraft are 5 miles apart and converging. The required separation is 3 miles. Do you need to issue a traffic advisory?

choices.text: Yes — in your judgment, their proximity may diminish below the 3-mile separation minimum.
No — they currently have 5 miles of separation.
No — advisories are only required within 2 miles.
Wait until they are at 3 miles before issuing.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-21: Issue traffic advisories when proximity MAY diminish below separation minima — proactive, not reactive.

items.target_phrase: TRAFFIC ELEVEN O CLOCK TEN MILES SOUTHBOUND CONVERGING BOEING SEVEN TWENTY SEVEN ONE SEVEN THOUSAND

items.question_text: When should advisory information about UAS activity be issued?

choices.text: When UAS activity is known, pilot-reported, or tower-observed and in your judgment the proximity warrants the advisory.
Only after a UAS causes an actual conflict with another aircraft.
Only when the UAS is operating above 400 feet AGL.
Only when the pilot specifically asks for unmanned traffic reports by name.

items.explanation: Known, pilot-reported, and tower-observed UAS activity can all trigger the advisory when proximity warrants it.

items.question_text: For pilot-reported or tower-observed UAS activity, controllers must continue issuing advisories to potentially impacted aircraft for at least 15 minutes following the last report.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The 15-minute continuation rule applies after the last pilot-reported or tower-observed report.

items.question_text: If UAS activity is known, controllers should include position, distance, course, type of unmanned aircraft, and altitude when those details are known and proximity warrants the advisory.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Those are the listed details for known UAS activity when available.

items.question_text: This UAS-advisory rule applies to known activity and to pilot-reported or tower-observed activity, with slightly different detail sets for each category.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Known activity and reported or observed activity are both covered, but the listed advisory details differ slightly.

items.situation: A pilot reports a drone 2 miles ahead at their altitude. Another aircraft is on the same route 5 miles behind. What must you do?

choices.text: Issue a UAS advisory to the trailing aircraft with the reported position, distance, and altitude.
No action needed — one report is not confirmed.
Close the airspace until the drone is located.
Wait for a second report before taking action.

items.situation: At 1405Z a pilot reports a flock of geese at 3,000 feet, 2 miles north. It is now 1418Z. An aircraft is about to depart on a route that will pass near the reported location. Must you issue an advisory?

choices.text: Yes — 15 minutes have not elapsed since the 1405Z report (1405 + 15 = 1420Z). The advisory requirement is still in effect.
No — 13 minutes is close enough to 15.
No — the geese have probably moved on by now.
Yes — but only because the aircraft is departing, not arriving.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-23(a): Bird activity advisories must be issued for at least 15 minutes after receipt of the report.

items.question_text: What information should a bird activity advisory include when known?

choices.text: Position, species or size, course of flight, and altitude.
Only the number of birds and the airport name.
Only the species, because pilots can judge the rest visually.
Position and runway in use, but not course or altitude.

items.situation: You are being relieved for a 15-minute break. The relieving controller has been working an adjacent position all shift and knows the traffic. Can you skip the full SOP transfer procedure since it's brief and the controller is experienced?

choices.text: No — the SOP transfer must be accomplished EACH time operational responsibility is transferred, regardless of duration or the relieving controller's experience.
Yes — the relieving controller is already familiar with the traffic.
Yes — transfers under 30 minutes don't require the full SOP.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-24: The SOP must be followed EACH time responsibility transfers. Duration and experience are not exceptions.

items.question_text: How must transfer of position responsibility be accomplished?

choices.text: In accordance with the Standard Operating Practice for Transfer of Position Responsibility and facility directives each time responsibility changes hands.
Only when two specialists disagree about the traffic picture.
At the outgoing specialist's discretion whenever workload is light.
Only when a radar handoff is involved.

items.explanation: Each transfer of operational responsibility follows the SOP and applicable facility directives.

items.situation: A USAF F-16 is on final approach. The pilot reported wheels down on the previous approach but went around. On this approach, the pilot has not mentioned wheels. Must you issue the wheels-down reminder?

choices.text: Yes — the previous report was for the prior approach. A new approach requires a new reminder unless the pilot has already reported for this approach.
No — the previous report covers all subsequent approaches.
No — USAF aircraft are exempt from wheels-down reminders.
Yes — but only because it's a fighter aircraft.

items.question_text: What is the purpose of a wheels-down check?

choices.text: To remind the pilot to lower the wheels, not to shift responsibility to the controller.
To certify that the landing gear is actually down and locked.
To transfer responsibility for landing gear verification from the pilot to ATC.
To replace the pilot's normal before-landing checklist.

items.question_text: Which kind of condition must be brought to the supervisor or controller-in-charge?

Only equipment outages that have already caused a loss of separation.

items.explanation: The order requires supervisors or CICs to be aware of a broad range of conditions affecting operations.

Only notify if a pilot declares an emergency.

items.situation: A pilot reports 'TAWS alert, pulling up.' You were about to issue a descent to that aircraft for traffic. What should you do?

choices.text: Do not issue the descent — it contradicts the TAWS pull-up maneuver. Support the alert and manage traffic around it.
Issue the descent — ATC separation takes priority over onboard alerts.
Tell the pilot to disregard the alert.
Issue the descent but at a reduced rate.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-30(a): Do not issue control instructions contrary to the TAWS procedure the crew is executing.

items.question_text: When does ATC responsibility for approved separation resume after an aircraft responds to a TAWS alert?

choices.text: When the aircraft returns to its assigned altitude, or the crew reports the maneuver complete and you observe separation reestablished, or the aircraft is on an alternate clearance and you observe separation reestablished.
Only after the aircraft lands and clears the runway.
As soon as the pilot acknowledges the last transmission, even if the maneuver is still in progress.
Only when the supervisor personally authorizes ATC to resume separation responsibility.

items.explanation: Separation responsibility resumes only after one of the specific recovery conditions in the paragraph has been met.

items.question_text: What should be done with a report of possible human trafficking, sometimes called a Blue Lightning event?

choices.text: Ensure the supervisor or controller-in-charge is notified.
Wait for confirmation from law enforcement before telling anyone in the facility.
Record it only in local notes unless an emergency is declared.
Pass it only to the next controller handling the aircraft and no one else.

items.explanation: Possible human trafficking reports are elevated to the supervisor or CIC.

items.situation: A flight attendant reports via the flight deck that a passenger may be a victim of human trafficking. The pilot relays this to you. What must you do?

choices.text: Ensure the supervisor/CIC is notified — this is a 'Blue Lightning' event requiring supervisory notification.
Contact law enforcement directly.
Tell the pilot to handle it on arrival.
No ATC action is required — this is an airline matter.

items.explanation: Per 2-1-31: Reports of possible human trafficking ('Blue Lightning' events) must be brought to the supervisor/CIC's attention.

items.question_text: Which transmissions are authorized on ATC communications channels?

choices.text: Only messages necessary for air traffic control or otherwise contributing to air safety.
Any operationally interesting information, even if it does not affect ATC or safety.
Routine company messages whenever the frequency is quiet.
Administrative traffic if all controllers on the position agree.

items.explanation: The paragraph limits transmissions to ATC necessity or air-safety contribution.

items.situation: The control frequency is quiet. An airline asks you to pass a routine gate-assignment update to one of its aircraft because dispatch cannot reach the crew another way.
items.para_context: Transmit only those messages necessary for air traffic control or otherwise contributing to air safety.

choices.text: Do not pass it on ATC frequency unless it is necessary for ATC or contributes to air safety.
Pass it because any quiet period on the frequency allows non-ATC company traffic.
Pass it only if the crew first says unable to contact company on another channel.
Pass it because anything involving an aircraft operator automatically counts as ATC business.

items.explanation: Frequency quietness is not the standard. Necessity for ATC or air safety is.

items.question_text: May a message that is not directly part of control instructions still be transmitted if it affects safe flight operations?

choices.text: It may be transmitted if it contributes to air safety.
It may not be transmitted unless it is worded as a clearance.
It may be transmitted only after the aircraft leaves controlled airspace.
It may be transmitted only if the pilot specifically requests it by call sign.

items.explanation: The paragraph expressly allows messages that otherwise contribute to air safety.

items.instruction: Build the correct phraseology from these tiles.
items.target_phrase: TRANSMIT ONLY MESSAGES NECESSARY FOR AIR TRAFFIC CONTROL
items.explanation: Per 2-4-5: Transmit only those messages necessary for ATC or contributing to air safety.

items.question_text: Which response best matches the required actions when false or deceptive communications occur?

choices.text: Correct the false information, alert aircraft in the affected area, collect pertinent information, and notify the operations supervisor.
Wait for a second false transmission before broadcasting anything so you do not create unnecessary alarm.
Change only the working frequency and continue normal operations unless a pilot reports confusion.
Tell aircraft to ignore all ATC instructions until the source is located.

items.explanation: The paragraph lays out a four-part response: correct, broadcast, collect, and notify.

items.instruction: Put the false-communication response steps in order.

steps.text: Broadcast an alert to aircraft operating on all frequencies within the affected area.
Collect pertinent information regarding the incident.
Notify the operations supervisor and report all relevant information pertaining to the incident.

items.explanation: The required flow in 2-4-6 is to correct first, then alert aircraft, then collect information, then notify the operations supervisor.

items.situation: False ATC instructions have been reported near Long Beach Airport. Build the warning broadcast.
items.target_phrase: ATTENTION ALL AIRCRAFT FALSE ATC INSTRUCTIONS HAVE BEEN RECEIVED IN THE AREA OF LONG BEACH AIRPORT EXERCISE EXTREME CAUTION ON ALL FREQUENCIES AND VERIFY INSTRUCTIONS

items.clearance: ALL AIRCRAFT ON FREQUENCY FALSE COMMUNICATIONS REPORTED DISREGARD LAST CLEARANCE

choices.text: Disregard last clearance, Skyhawk 123AB
Roger, Skyhawk 123AB
Say again last clearance, 3AB
Unable to comply, 3AB

items.explanation: Per 2-4-6: When false communications are detected, broadcast an alert and correct false information.

items.question_text: When relaying operational information to an aircraft or aircraft operator, what two limitations apply?

choices.text: Give the source of the message and do not agree to handle such relays on a regular basis.
Give the source only if asked, and handle the relays regularly once the operator has validated them.
Treat the message as your own and avoid identifying the source to reduce confusion.
Relay it only if it comes from another ATC facility and never if it comes from an operator.

items.explanation: Necessary relays are allowed, but the source must be given and regular handling should not be accepted.

items.situation: An airline repeatedly asks your position to pass routine operational messages to one of its aircraft whenever dispatch is busy. The controller taking the request wants to just make it a normal service the position provides.
items.para_context: Relay operational information as necessary, do not agree to handle such messages on a regular basis, and give the source of any such message you relay.

choices.text: Relay only as necessary, give the source, and do not agree to handle the messages on a regular basis.
Agree to make it a standard service because the messages are operational and the airline uses your airspace often.
Refuse every relay request, because controllers may never relay operator-originated information.
Relay the message but remove the source so the flight crew treats it as direct ATC guidance.

items.explanation: The paragraph permits necessary relays but expressly forbids agreeing to handle them routinely.

items.question_text: Which additional relay categories may be relayed in addition to as-needed operational information for aircraft or operators?

choices.text: Official FAA messages as required, and operational information to military aircraft operating on or planning to operate on IRs.
Only military messages, because official FAA messages must always go by administrative channels.
Only official FAA messages, because IR-related traffic is handled entirely by flight service.
Neither of those; 2-4-7 is limited strictly to civil operator traffic.

items.explanation: Subparagraphs b and c identify those additional relay categories directly.

items.explanation: Per 2-4-7(a): When relaying operational messages, give the source of the message.

items.question_text: When may gate or fix names be substituted for interphone position identification?

choices.text: Only when the substitution is operationally beneficial and covered by an LOA or facility directive.
Any time the caller believes a gate or fix name will be shorter.
Only between positions in the same building, with no additional documentation needed.
Only when neither position uses a numeric or titled line name.

items.explanation: Alternative interphone identifiers are allowed only under controlled, documented conditions.

choices.text: Restart with a clear facility and/or position identification that avoids confusion.
Continue with the shorthand nickname because speed matters more than distinct identification.
Use any gate or fix name even without an LOA if both controllers think it is obvious.
Skip position identification entirely and move straight to the coordination message.

items.explanation: The format begins with clear caller/receiver identification, not an ambiguous local shortcut.

items.clearance: PODUNK GROUND PODUNK TOWER INTERPHONE CHECK

choices.text: Podunk Tower, Podunk Ground, interphone check received.
Loud and clear, Ground.
Roger, Tower.
Interphone operational, out.

items.explanation: Per 2-4-12(a): Both caller and receiver identify their facility/position on interphone.

items.question_text: When may an en route controller omit the words super or heavy?

choices.text: They may be omitted en route except in the specifically listed cases where they still must be used.
They may always be omitted once radar contact is established.
They may never be omitted in any en route communication.
They may be omitted only when the pilot requests shorter phraseology.

items.explanation: The paragraph allows limited en route omission, not universal omission.

items.instruction: Build the correct phraseology from these tiles.
items.target_phrase: USE WORDS AND PHRASES AS CONTAINED IN THE PILOT CONTROLLER GLOSSARY
items.explanation: Per 2-4-14(a): The P/CG is the authoritative source for standard radiotelephone and interphone phraseology.

items.situation: You are coordinating with a terminal facility about a heavy aircraft operation. Another controller wants to omit the word heavy because the discussion is taking place in the en route environment.
items.para_context: En route omission of super or heavy is limited and does not apply in every communication, including all listed exceptions such as communications with or about certain terminal operations and traffic advisories. Air Force One and Air Force Two are not given the heavy designator.

choices.text: Use the heavy designator if the communication falls within one of the listed exceptions instead of omitting it automatically.
Omit heavy because any en route controller may omit it in every communication.
Replace heavy with super because the aircraft is already known to be large.
Add heavy even to Air Force One if the aircraft weight supports it.

items.explanation: Omission is conditional, not universal, and presidential call signs are a separate exception.

items.question_text: Which pronunciation standard should be used for numbers and individual letters?

choices.text: ICAO pronunciation.
Any local pronunciation if the pilot sounds domestic.
FAA-only pronunciation for numbers and ICAO only for letters.
Plain conversational English instead of a standard phonetic system.

items.explanation: Numbers and individual letters use the ICAO pronunciation standard.

items.question_text: Which pronunciation must be used for numbers and individual letters?

choices.text: ICAO phonetics.
Local pronunciation whenever the pilot and controller share the same first language.
Military phonetics for letters and ICAO only for numbers.
Any clear pronunciation as long as the word is spoken slowly.

items.explanation: The standard is fixed: use ICAO phonetics for numbers and individual letters.

items.situation: A controller is tempted to use plain-English letter names and casual number pronunciation because the pilot is domestic and the exchange seems simple.
items.para_context: Numbers and individual letters use ICAO phonetics.

choices.text: Use ICAO phonetics for the numbers and individual letters instead of switching to casual local pronunciation.
Switch to plain-English pronunciation because the pilot is domestic and already understands the message.
Use local pronunciation for letters only and ICAO for numbers.
Use conversational pronunciation whenever the call sign contains fewer than four characters.

items.explanation: The standard does not depend on how easy the exchange feels; it remains ICAO phonetics.

items.clearance: SKYHAWK THREE ALFA BRAVO SQUAWK ONE TWO ZERO ZERO

choices.text: Squawk one two zero zero, Skyhawk 3AB.
Twelve hundred, 3AB.
One two zero zero, roger.
Squawk twelve hundred, Skyhawk 123AB.

items.explanation: Per 2-4-16: ICAO pronunciation requires each digit spoken separately — 'ONE TWO ZERO ZERO.'

items.question_text: When may a controller restate a number in a different form?

choices.text: After first stating it the standard way, if extra clarity is needed.
Any time the controller prefers group form over the standard wording.
Only when the pilot specifically asks for the number to be repeated twice.
Before giving the standard form, if the alternate form sounds shorter.

items.explanation: Number clarification comes after the standard 2-4-17 wording and only when needed for clarity.

items.instruction: Build the correct phraseology from these tiles.
items.target_phrase: RESTATE NUMBERS FOR CLARITY USING GROUP OR SINGLE DIGIT FORM
items.explanation: Per 2-4-18(a): Numbers may be restated in group or single-digit form when needed for clarity.

items.situation: You assign 17,000 feet as "one seven thousand." The pilot sounds unsure and asks for confirmation.
items.para_context: If clarity requires it, a number already stated under 2-4-17 may be restated in either group form or single-digit form.

choices.text: Restate it for clarity, such as "one seven thousand, seventeen thousand."
Repeat only "seventeen" because the word thousand is implied.
Switch permanently to group form and stop using the standard 2-4-17 wording at all.
Refuse to restate it because the standard form may never be followed by a different form.

items.explanation: A clarification restatement is allowed after the standard form when it helps resolve confusion.

items.question_text: How should you reply to aircraft with similar sounding identifications?

choices.text: Use the full identification.
Use the same abbreviation both pilots used on initial callup.
Use only the last two digits or letters to keep transmissions shorter.
Use the aircraft type alone until one leaves the frequency.

items.explanation: Similar-sounding aircraft require full identification in replies.

items.question_text: Which prefix must be stated when establishing initial communications with a U.S. registered civil aircraft?

choices.text: November.
Tango.
Medevac.
Executive.

choices.text: Use the full identification in replies to the similar-sounding aircraft.
Keep the abbreviations because once communications are established abbreviation takes priority over clarity.
Drop both call signs and use aircraft type only until one aircraft changes frequency.
Use the pilot's first name or company name instead of the filed identification.

items.explanation: Clarity controls the reply when aircraft identifications sound alike.

American Twenty Three Heavy, Tower.

items.question_text: Outside EAS FDP automatic forwarding, what should be done with flight plan information?

choices.text: Forward it to the appropriate ATC facility, FSS, or BASOPS and record the time of filing and delivery on the form.
Keep it locally until the next facility requests it by interphone.
Forward it only to ATC facilities and not to FSS or BASOPS.
Record only the time of filing, since delivery time will always be automatic.

items.explanation: Manual forwarding includes both the transmission target and the requirement to record filing and delivery times.

items.question_text: Except during EAS FDP operation, controllers must forward flight plan information to the appropriate ATC facility, FSS, or BASOPS and record the time of filing and delivery on the form.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is the baseline forwarding rule when EAS FDP automation is not handling the exchange.

items.question_text: If EAS FDP is operating but the data is not forwarded automatically by the computer, en route controllers must perform the manual forwarding actions.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: The forwarding responsibility for proposed tower en route flight plans and related amendments belongs to the appropriate departure terminal facility.

choices.text: True
False

items.situation: You need to forward a flight plan to the receiving facility. What two pieces of information must you record on the form?

choices.text: The time of filing and the time of delivery.
Only the time of filing.
Only the aircraft's departure time.
The time of filing and the aircraft's destination.

items.explanation: Per 2-2-2(a): Record the time of filing AND delivery on the form.

items.question_text: When must terminal controllers forward aircraft departure times for VFR flights?

choices.text: When FSSs or military operations offices have requested them.
